info.parenttitle: 3.2 Variations in economic activity - aggregate demand and aggregate supply

markdown:
<definition term="Aggregate demand">The **total** **quantity **of **real output** that **all** **buyers** in an economy (consumers, businesses, the government, and foreigners) are **willing** **to** **purchase** over a **specific** **time** **period**, at **all** **possible** **price** **levels**, _**ceteris** **paribus**_.</definition>

1. Aggregate Demand (AD) is not just the demand of real output from consumers.
2. AD consists of the total demand for goods and services in an economy across all the expenditure components:
  1. **Consumer spending (C)**: all **spending** made by **households** on **final** **goods** and **services** during a particular time period. 
  2. **Investment (I)**: investments made:
      1. **By firms on physical capital:** money spent on **assets** used to produce goods and services (factories, machinery, offices...).
    2. **By households and firms on new construction**: new housing and other new construction **assets**.** **
  3. **Government spending (G)**: spending made by governments on the economy (roads, hospitals, purchasing of factors of production...).
  4. **Net exports (X-M)**: the market value of the **exports** (X) of an economy **minus** the market value of **imports** (M) into an economy.
      1. **Exports **refer to goods and services **produced** **domestically**, and so are **included** in the calculation of the **national output** (**GDP**).
    2. **Imports** represent **domestic** **spending** on goods and services **produced** in **external** **economies**, and so must be subtracted from expenditures to accurately measure **domestic** **output**.

Therefore, the formula for Aggregate Demand (AD):

$$AD=C+I+G+(X-M)$$

<callout type="tip">Aggregate Demand (AD) can be understood as the real GDP (real output) that consumers, firms, governments, and foreigners want to buy at each possible price level (over a period of time, _ceteris paribus_).

It is for this reason that AD uses the same formula as real GDP; it adds up the amount of real output that each GDP component is willing to buy at each price level.</callout>

<callout type="note">The **price level** is nothing more than the **average** of **all** **prices** of **goods** and **services** in an **economy**. It shows how **expensive** or **cheap** things are overall.</callout>

## Aggregate demand (AD) curve


The AD curve (_Figure 1_) visually represents the relationship between the **price** **level** (vertical axis) and **real GDP** (horizontal axis).

![Figure 1: Aggregate demand curve.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b8f87a555edf3654b597613b77f264326fc2a783-3600x2914.png)

As can be seen by _Figure 1_ above, the **AD curve** is **downward-sloping**. Therefore:

1. As the **Price Level** (PL) of an economy **increases**, the **aggregate demand** for real output of all buyers (consumers, firms, government, and foreigners) **decreases**.
2. As the **Price Level** (PL) of an economy **decreases**, the **aggregate demand increases**.

<callout type="note">The **negative** **relationship** between the Price Level (PL) and Aggregate Demand (AD), is due to two effects:

1. **The wealth effect**: 
  1. At **lower** **price** **levels**, the **purchasing** **power** of money **increases**, making **consumers** **feel** **wealthier**. 
  2. This **increases** the amount of **real** **GDP** consumers are **willing** **to** **consume**.
2. **The interest rate effect**:
  1. **Lower** **price** **levels** lead to **lower** **interest** **rates**. 
  2. With **lower** **interest** **rates**, firms can borrow money for cheaper; **increasing **the real GDP **firms **are willing to buy as **investments**.
  3. With **lower interest rates**, consumers are less encouraged to save (since they gain less interest in saving accounts), which the amount of real GDP **consumer** are willing to buy.

These two reasons behind the AD curve being downward-slopping are beyond the scope of the IB Economics syllabus.</callout>
